summary: Justin Brennan walks through the financial due diligence process for multifamily acquisitions in escrow, covering the critical documents and reports needed to validate property economics during the 20-30 day due diligence window. He emphasizes matching rent rolls to actual leases, analyzing T12 profit & loss statements, and segregating deferred maintenance from value-add improvements to avoid overpaying for a property.
takeaways: Conduct financial, physical, and market due diligence within a 20-30 day escrow window before closing
Request rent roll (tenant list, unit types, square footage, market rents, current rents, vacancies) and audit every single lease to verify occupancy matches
Obtain T12 (12-month P&L broken down monthly) showing gross rents, concessions, loss to lease, vacancies, and all operating expenses; verify against bank statements and tax returns
Request 12 months of utility bills, all vendor/service contracts (landscaping, pest control, trash, pool maintenance), and aged receivables report to identify delinquent tenants
Get unit availability report showing current vacancies and lease move-out notices for next couple months
Separate and quantify deferred maintenance (HVAC, roof, windows, drainage, plumbing, electrical that don't add rent value) from improvement capex—very important for capitalizing costs

Request capex/repair expense verification from property management and verify RUBs (utility reimbursements) match other income; request 24 months of work orders showing repair history and response time

skill_candidates.change: Create a systematic verification process matching rent rolls to leases and T12 statements to bank statements/tax returns before closing
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin emphasizes 'match that up to the actual leases,' 'do what's called a lease audit,' and 'getting that backed up through bank statements through tax returns'—this prevents discovering tenant/occupancy problems post-close when 'buyer beware' applies

skill_candidates.change: Quantify and segregate deferred maintenance costs from value-add capex in underwriting models
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin states 'Very, very, very important' twice about distinguishing deferred maintenance (required repairs with no rent upside like HVAC/roof/electrical) from improvements, emphasizing 'you have to capitalize that up front' and potentially negotiate seller credit

title: Lesson 1: Financial Due Diligence
